pages.text: The anisotropy of cracked concrete was considered in the model developed by Li et al. [19]. The inner cracked cylinder was modelled as an orthotropic material with the modulus of elasticity reduced only in the tangential direction normal to cracks, while the modulus of elasticity in the radial direction remained unchanged and was equal to that in the outer uncracked cylinder. The reduced modulus of elasticity was calculated by averaging the tangential strains in the inner cylinder. Therefore, the modulus was constant throughout the cylinder wall though it was noted that the tangential stiffness in the inner cylinder had to be a function of the radial coordinate. This for- mulation cannot provide compatibility in both stresses and strains on the common boundary between the two cylinders. It is also important to note that following Bazant[14] all the above mentioned models have used plane stress solutions. However, it is only correct for an internally pressurised hollow cylinder with free ends while the concrete cylinder around a reinforcing bar is actually within the bulk concrete of a RC element, which prevents free defor- mation in the direction of the cylinder axis. Under such conditions, the use of a plane strain solution is much more appropriate. An improved analytical model based on a thick-walled double cylinder was proposed by Chernin et al. [20]. Cracks in the inner cylinder were considered as smeared. Linearly elastic material rep- resenting cracked concrete of the inner cylinder was modelled as cylindrically anisotropic (or more exactly as inhomogeneously orthotropic). The degree of anisotropy in the inner cylinder was varied depending on the radial coordinate, r, by changing the stiff- ness in the tangential direction in accordance with the following power function f ðrÞ¼ r Rc /C18/C19 n ð24Þ Since Eq. (24) leads to f(r = Rc) = 1, the inﬂuence of anisotropy disappears at the common boundary between the cylinders, i.e., at r = Rc (see Fig. 2 b). As a result, complete compatibility in both stress and strain (displacement) ﬁelds can be achieved between the inner and outer cylinders. A more detailed description of the model is presented in Appendix. Calibration of the model (i.e., evaluation of the exponent n) was performed using results of the tests carried out by Williamson and Clark [52], in which cracking of the concrete cover was caused by controlled pressure applied within cylindrical holes made in con- crete specimens. The obtained values of n were widely scattered; however, it was shown that the lowest possible value of n was 1.5, which could be used as a tentative estimate. 4. Existing numerical models for prediction of corrosion- induced cover cracking A number of numerical models for the prediction of corrosion- induced cover cracking have been proposed. A simple numerical model of concrete cover cracking caused by corrosion of reinforc- ing steel was presented by Pantazopoulou and Papoulia [22].I n fact, it represented an improved thick-walled cylinder model with a partially cracked internal part. As noted previously, the main weakness of many analytical models, which consider the partition of the cylinder into cracked and uncracked parts, is either their inconsistence or the lack of compatibility at the boundary between the cylinder parts. Pantazopoulou and Papoulia [22] resolved these problems by taking into account a gradual change of the concrete secant stiffness along the wall of the inner cylinder in accordance

concrete. Thus, to formulate its model parameters characterising deformability and shear behaviour of corrosion products need to be deﬁned. Up until now, only a few experimental studies have investigated the mechanical behaviour of rust [48,58–60]. Accord- ing to Samsonov [61], the modulus of elasticity of iron oxide crys- tals (Fe2O3 or Fe3O4) is in the range from 215 to 350 GPa. However, these values cannot be used directly for corrosion products be- cause of their granular nature, which results in a signiﬁcant reduc- tion of their stiffness. Yoshioka and Yonezawa [58] measured expansive strains in steel plates subjected to galvanostatic corro- sion and conﬁned by concrete bars. Based on these results Suda et al. [49] estimated that the modulus of elasticity of corrosion products was in the range from 0.1 to 0.5 GPa. Ouglova et al. [48] using a number of techniques found that as the degree of com- paction increased the modulus of elasticity of rust increased non- linearly from 1–2 GPa (e.g., the tangent modulus measured from mechanical testing was 1.26 GPa at compressive stress of 5 MPa) up to several hundred GPa (the value for pure oxide crystals); such behaviour is typical for granular materials. Normal compressive stresses acting within the rust layer at the time of concrete cover cracking can be roughly estimated using Eq. (14) as equal to the value of internal pressure, i.e., P cr. The corre- sponding compressive strains can be found from experimental re- sults on deformability of rust [48]. For example, for the high c/d ratio of 5 and fct = 3 MPa Eq. (14) yields Pcr = 30 MPa and the corre- sponding compressive strain in the rust layer is then less than 0.10 [48]. However, as shows Fig. 4 the TWUC model signiﬁcantly over- estimates Pcr for high c/d ratios due to its inability to account for nonlinear behaviour of concrete after cracking. Thus, actual com- pressive strains in the rust layer are much lower than 0.10 so that a relative decrease in the thickness of the rust layer at the time of cover cracking should be much less than 10%. This means that error in evaluating the increase in the rebar diameter at cover cracking (i.e., Ddcr) due to neglecting deformability of the rust layer should also be much less than 10% that seems to be acceptable. In this re- spect it is necessary to mention the assessment of deformability of rust made by Lundgren [34], which was based on ﬁtting estimates of the corrosion penetration causing cover cracking obtained numerically to the corresponding values observed in tests. Although the qualitative conclusion of this study that the rust behaviour was similar to that of a granular material was correct, its quantitative results overestimated signiﬁcantly deformability of corrosion products. For example, at compressive stress of 5 MPa according to the results of Lundgren [34] the corresponding strain was 0.32, while in the tests carried out by Ouglova et al. [48] it was only 0.04. The rust layer also transfers by friction shear stresses between a corroded reinforcing bar and the surrounding concrete, which is described by using the Coulomb friction model. This is imple- mented in the FE model by employing the penalty contact algo- rithm in ABAQUS [56]. Lundgren [34] suggested that the coefﬁcient of friction between the two materials varied from 1.0 for undamaged concrete to 0.4 for cracked and crushed concrete. Cairns et al. [60] investigated the friction characteristics of the con- crete-corroded stee

case is obtained; however, in this case the difference between the TWDC and FE results in Section 6 increases. To increase one error to offset another one does not look like a proper way to deal with the problem. In fact, the above discussion leads to an interesting conclusion that an improvement of analytical models based on a thick-walled cylinder in terms of description of the nonlinear behaviour of concrete compared with the simple TWUC model may not be beneﬁcial. In many cases it may result in larger errors compared with the TWUC results when corrosion-induced cover cracking in real RC structures is analysed. Finally, it is necessary to note that validity of the use of the sin- gle bar CB and MB models shown in Fig. 14 for examining cracking around corner and middle reinforcing bars has been checked using larger models which include two and three reinforcing bars. Com- parison of results yielded by the models has shown that the single bar models provide sufﬁciently accurate results for both types of concrete cover failure, i.e., cracking and delamination, in the con- sidered ranges of the c 1/d and c2/c1 ratios. 8. Thickness of the porous zone As has been noted previously, a part of corrosion products pen- etrates into concrete pores and cracks and, therefore, does not con- tribute to pressure build up between concrete and corroding reinforcement. This phenomenon has been considered as the main reason that analytical and numerical models signiﬁcantly underes- timate the time to cover cracking. In order to take this into account Liu and Weyers [4] suggested to assume that there was a porous zone around a reinforcing bar and corrosion products did not exert any pressure on the surrounding concrete until they fully ﬁlled this zone. They also suggested that the thickness of the porous zone, d0, which became one of the main parameters controlling cover crack- ing, could be estimated indirectly by ﬁtting the times to cover cracking obtained analytically to those observed in experiments. Using this approach, their own experimental data, and their erro- neous solution Liu and Weyers [4] estimated d0 as 12.5 lm. Petre-Lazar and Gérard [59] investigated the concrete–rebar inter- face using a scanning electron microscope. Based on this study he proposed a formula relating d0 with the thickness of the concrete– rebar interface (set as 40 lm), the degree of hydration, and the water–cement ratio. According to the formula, d0 varies between 2 lm and 8 lm depending on the values of the degree of hydration and the water–cement ratio. El Maaddawy and Soudki [15] consid- ered d0 in the range of 10–20 lm; however, in their analysis they signiﬁcantly underestimated the effective modulus of elasticity of concrete. The thickness of the porous zone is estimated in this study using the approach proposed by Liu and Weyers [4], i.e., the in- crease in the rebar diameter corresponding to cover cracking ob- tained numerically (from FE analysis), Ddcr,FEA, equals that from experiments, Ddcr,exp, minus 2 d0 so that d0 ¼ 1 2 ðDdcr;exp /C0 Ddcr;FEAÞð 30Þ Ddcr,exp is calculated by Eqs. (26) and (27) based on the times to cov- er cracking from a number of accelerated corrosion tests [2,7,8,37– 40]. To evaluate Ddcr,FEA the FE analysis is employed that allows increasing the accuracy of estimations compared to analytical mod- elling. The effective modulus of elasticity of concrete Ec,ef = Ec/ (1 + ut) used in the FE analyses is evaluated for the times of cover crackin

Prediction of corrosion-induced cover cracking in reinforced concrete structures Leon Chernin ⇑, Dimitri V. Val School of the Built Environment, Heriot-Watt University, Edinburgh EH14 4AS, UK article info Article history: Received 11 February 2010 Received in revised form 4 October 2010 Accepted 13 November 2010 Available online 8 December 2010 Keywords: Corrosion Concrete Reinforcing steel Cracking Delamination Empirical models Analytical models Finite element model abstract Usually, the time of repair/replacement of reinforced concrete structures due to corrosion is controlled by cracking of the concrete cover. Thus, it is important to be able to predict with sufﬁcient accuracy the time from corrosion initiation to crack formation in the concrete cover. The paper presents a critical overview of existing empirical, analytical and numerical models for predicting the time to corrosion-induced cover cracking. A simple nonlinear ﬁnite element (FE) is then formulated and validated. The FE model is employed to investigate inaccuracies of analytical models arising due to simpliﬁed formulation of the nonlinear behaviour of concrete and inability to account for actual location of corroding reinforcing bars. Recommendations on the applicability of analytical models are provided. Finally, the FE model is used to estimate the amount of corrosion products penetrating into concrete pores and microcracks since this amount is an essential parameter for analytical and numerical models. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Reinforced concrete (RC) structures often suffer from damage due to corrosion of embedded reinforcing steel caused by carbon- ation or chloride contamination of concrete [1]. The main indicator of corrosion presence is cracking of the concrete cover. Usually, excessive cracking, which affects normal performance of a struc- ture, appears before corrosion has any signiﬁcant inﬂuence on strength of the structure [2,3]. As a result, the time for structural repair/replacement due to corrosion is usually controlled by ser- viceability limit states associated with corrosion-induced cracking of the concrete cover [4,5]. Cracks immediately after their initiation are very small (hairline cracks of width less than 0.05 mm) and generally do not represent immediate danger for serviceability of a structure. However, they can lead to acceleration of the structure deterioration so that the time of their initiation is a major charac- teristic of structural performance and it is important to be able to predict it with sufﬁcient accuracy. A large number of models have been proposed to predict the time to crack initiation, which can be broadly divided into the three main groups: (i) empirical models [6–13], (ii) analytical mod- els [4,14–21], and (iii) numerical models [22–36]. Most the models have been developed and/or calibrated/validated based on limited experimental data. This does not provide sufﬁcient information for identifying the most accurate and efﬁcient models and, conse- quently, creates problems for practitioners in selecting appropriate models for estimating the time to corrosion-induced cracking. Thus, the aim of this paper is to provide a critical overview of the existing models and formulate recommendations on the model selection. This is achieved by comparing predictions of the models with available experimental data and results of nonlinear ﬁnite element analysis (for analytical models) as well as by examining

location of corroding reinforcing bars in a RC section (i.e., the actual boundary conditions). In order to estimate the inﬂuence of errors arising from the ﬁrst source, values of the internal pressure, which causes cracking of the cylinder wall, are calculated using the ana- lytical models and the FE analysis for a number of concrete hollow cylinders with different ratios of the wall thickness to the internal diameter. The effect of errors from the second source is assessed by carrying out nonlinear FE analysis of two segments of a RC slab, which represent conditions of a reinforcing bar in the corner and in the middle of the slab. The FE analysis also enables to take into account friction between reinforcing bars and the surrounding concrete. Results obtained by the FE analysis are then compared with those yielded by the analytical models for concrete hollow cylinders with the wall thickness equal to the thickness of the con- crete cover in the RC slab. Recommendations on the use of analyt- ical/numerical models depending on the ratio of the concrete cover thickness to the diameter of a reinforcing bar and the bar location are presented. Another important factor, which signiﬁcantly affects both ana- lytical and numerical prediction of corrosion-induced cracking of a concrete cover, is the penetration of corrosion products into con- crete pores and microcracks that reduces pressure between a cor- roding reinforcing bar and the surrounding concrete. The amount of corrosion products penetrating into concrete pores and micro- cracks is estimated by ﬁtting results of nonlinear FE analysis to available experimental data. Obtained results show that the amount of penetrating corrosion products is higher than it has pre- viously been assumed. Moreover, the paper also demonstrates that a generally accepted approach to account for the penetration of corrosion products into concrete pores and microcracks, which is based on the assumption of a porous zone around a reinforcing bar [1], leads to results that cannot be explained from a physical point of view. 2. Existing empirical models for prediction of corrosion- induced cover cracking Empirical models are based on the analysis of experimental re- sults, which includes determination of major parameters control- ling corrosion-induced cracking and then derivation by a regression analysis of a formula relating these parameters with a critical amount of corrosion products needed to cause cracking (or directly with the time to cover cracking). The major parameters normally considered in the empirical models are geometrical prop- erties such as the thickness of the concrete cover, c, and the diam- eter of a reinforcing bar, d, and properties of concrete. The simplest empirical model depending on a single parameter – c, was proposed by Webster and Clark [6]. According to this mod- el, the critical corrosion penetration (i.e., the corrosion penetration corresponding to cracking of the concrete cover), x cr, is linearly pro- portional to c xcr ¼ 1:25c ð1Þ where xcr is in lm and c in mm. A number of the models relate the critical amount of corrosion products with the c/d ratio. Andrade et al. [7] suggested that for c/ d < 2 cracking of the concrete cover occurs at xcr =2 0 lm. Alonso et al. [8] found a liner relationship between xcr (in lm) and c/d xcr ¼ 7:53 þ 9:32 c d ð2Þ A more complex relationship relating the critical mass of corro- sion products per unit surface area of a reinforcing bar, W0 rust;cr ,

against results of the tests carried out by Williamson and Clark [52], in which cracking of the concrete cover was caused by con- trolled pressure applied within holes made in concrete specimens (the pressure was provided by a hydraulic jack through soft tubes inserted into the holes). Parameters varied in the tests included the diameter of the holes, d, the thickness of the concrete cover, c, and the tensile strength of concrete, fct. Results of the comparison are shown in Fig. 9 . As can be seen there is a reasonably good agree- ment between the test and analytical results. It is necessary to note that a ﬁnite element is assumed to be cracked when the elastic lim- it (i.e., the peak of the stress-displacement curve describing the concrete behaviour in tension) is reached. The failure pressure in the FE analysis is deﬁned as the pressure corresponding to onset of cracking in all the elements along the developing crack. Although results provided by the model cannot be compared quantitatively with results of tests involving actual corrosion, they have been compared qualitatively with results of accelerated cor- rosion tests of RC slabs [39]. The process of cracking has been investigated in the part of the slab shown in Fig. 5 . Cracks formed in the slab in the test are shown in Fig. 10. Results of the nonlinear FE analysis are shown in Fig. 11 for different stages of expansion around the reinforcing bars (letters a–e indicate the order of in- crease in the expansion). As can be seen initially vertical cracks ap- pear in the concrete cover, followed by the formation of a horizontal crack between the reinforcing bars and delamination of the concrete cover. There is a good agreement between the crack Fig. 8. If to neglect (2d0 + Dd)2 (since 2 d0 + Dd /C28 d) then instead of Eq. (9) Dd can be found from the following formula: Dd ¼ 2Wrust pd 1 qrust /C0 c qsteel /C18/C19 /C0 2d0 ð16Þ To increase the estimated time to crack initiation compared to that yielded by the Bazant’s model [14] the relationship between expansion Dd and pressure P used by Liu and Weyers [4] was solely based on the solution for a hollow thick-walled cylinder, i.e., Eq. (11), that gave a reduced value P for the same value of Dd. The pressure causing the concrete cover cracking was estimated as in the Bazant’s model by Eq. (14). It is necessary to note that in the Liu and Weyers model [4] the internal diameter of a hollow cylin- der equals ( d +2 d0) and, therefore, in a rigorous analysis this expression should replace d in Eqs. (11) and (14) (and that was done in [4]). However, since 2 d0 /C28 d the use of Eqs. (11) and (14), as they are, leads to a negligible error. Finally, in order to further increase the estimated time to crack initiation Liu and Weyers [4] assumed that the rate of rust produc- tion, Jrust, was not constant but inversely proportional to the amount of corrosion products and, hence, decreased with time (similar assumption was used for atmospheric corrosion, e.g., Fontana [43]) Jrust ðtÞ¼ dWrust dt ¼ kp Wrust ð17Þ where kp is a constant. Solution of this differential equation is Wrust ðtÞ¼ ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ 2kpt q ð18Þ If the critical mass of corrosion products (i.e., the mass causing crack initiation), Wrust,cr, which can be found from Eqs. (11), (14), and (16), is known then the time from corrosion initiation to cover cracking, tcr, can then be estimated as tcr ¼ W2 rust;cr 2kp ð19Þ Substitution of the solution given by Eq. (18) into Eq. (17) also al- lows establishing a relationship between Jrust and t Jrust ðtÞ¼ ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ kp 2t r ð20Þ i.e., the rate of rust production is inversely proportional to the square root of the time from corrosion initiation. Using Faraday’s law of electrolysis dWsteel dt ¼ Icorr ðtÞM zF ð21Þ where Icorr is the corrosion current, M the molar mass of iron (55.85 g/mol), z the valence of the reaction (2 for Fe ? Fe2++2e/C0 ), and F Faraday’s constant (96,458 C/mol), and taking into account that Wsteel = cWrust, Jrust can be also expressed via the corrosion cur- rent density, icor = Icorr/(pd) (for a unit length of a reinforcing bar), as Jrust ðtÞ¼ M zF pd c icorr ðtÞð 22Þ The last result shows that icorr is directly proportional to Jrust and, subsequently, in the case when the assumption described by Eq. (17) is adopted, also inversely proportional to the square root of the time from corrosion initiation (Eq. (20)). As can be seen from Eq. (19) in order to estimate tcr a value of kp needs to be determined. This can be done using experimental re- sults, e.g., data showing the growth of Wrust with time (e.g., [44,45]). However, Liu and Weyers [4] did not determine kp cor- rectly. They denoted kp as the rate of rust production and then ex- pressed it via the corrosion current density as kp ¼ k pd c icorr ð23Þ where k is a constant, whose value depends on the units of the parameters appearing in the equations.
